title: The Practical Deep Learning Workflow
concept: A practical deep-learning workflow moves from problem framing to baseline, tracked experiments, controlled comparisons, and reproducible reruns, so the final model is not just impressive once but trustworthy and stable.

options.text: It turns ideas into repeatable steps that produce stable evidence.
It skips measurement so work feels faster.
It depends on one lucky run and no notes.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Practical workflow means the team can move from idea to trustworthy result in a repeatable way.

cards.title: Good projects do not run on vibes alone
cards.body: A beginner often imagines model building as “pick a network and hope.” In practice, strong teams follow a workflow: define the problem, create a baseline, track experiments, test one change at a time, and confirm the result can be repeated.

cards.title: The workflow as a checklist
cards.body: A practical deep-learning workflow is an end-to-end process for moving from idea to stable result. A simple version is: define the task clearly, choose a baseline, run tracked experiments, use ablations to learn what matters, and verify reproducibility before trusting the final outcome.

cards.title: Build the house in an order that makes sense
cards.body: You do not install curtains before pouring the foundation. Likewise, you should not celebrate a final architecture before you have a baseline, records, and repeatable results. Good workflow is partly about doing the right steps in the right order.

cards.title: A factory vision project from start to stability
cards.body: Imagine a factory wants to detect defective parts. The team first defines the labels and metric, then trains a simple baseline, then logs several improved runs, then ablates augmentation and model depth, and finally reruns the strongest setup with saved configs. That sequence turns “we tried a model” into “we have a stable system we understand.”

cards.title: The workflow is not bureaucracy for its own sake
cards.body: A common myth is that checklists, tracking, and reproducibility are just admin overhead. But they exist because deep learning is noisy. Without structure, teams waste time repeating mistakes, misreading gains, or trusting results that disappear on the next rerun.

cards.title: How the practical pieces connect
cards.body: Each step supports the next one.

left.text:
Jump straight to fancy models.
Weak records and unclear comparisons.
Hard to know what to trust.

right.text:
Baseline anchors the task.
Tracked runs and ablations teach what works.
Reproducibility confirms the final result.

exercises.prompt: Which step should usually come before celebrating a complex new model?

options.text: Build and measure a baseline first.
Delete old results so nothing can be compared.
Assume one run is enough proof.

exercises.explain: Right. A practical workflow starts with a reference point before chasing complexity.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Tracking, ablation, and reproducibility are connected parts of one practical workflow.
exercises.explain: True. They work together to make model progress understandable and reliable.

exercises.sentence: A practical workflow aims for a result that is not just exciting once, but also ___.
exercises.explain: Stable is correct. The goal is a result you can trust and repeat.

exercises.prompt: Which team sounds closest to the full practical workflow?

options.text: They define the metric, train a baseline, track runs, test components one at a time, and rerun the best setup.
They skip the baseline, skip logging, and trust whichever run feels lucky.
They add more layers every day without comparing to anything.

exercises.explain: Exactly. That sequence is what turns deep learning from trial-and-error into a disciplined workflow.

exercises.prompt: A team wants a dependable model for production. Which plan best matches the practical deep-learning workflow?

options.text: Define the task, build a baseline, track experiments, run ablations, and confirm the best setup is reproducible.
Start with the fanciest architecture, keep few notes, and trust the highest single score.
Change many things at once and skip reruns because they take time.
Treat baseline, tracking, and reproducibility as unrelated optional extras.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The practical workflow is a chain of habits that turns good ideas into stable evidence.
